hub_subtitle: Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

lesson.menu_title: Reporting a Problem -- Being Clear & Precise
lesson.menu_desc: Ana writes and delivers a clear incident report to stakeholders after a bug is found in production -- describing the impact, the symptom and the frequency, and using the passive voice to report what was done -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 10 -- Reporting a Problem
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 10 -- Reporting a Problem

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: how badly a problem affects users or the business
a visible sign that something is wrong
to fix a problem and close it
when something that worked before breaks after a change
able to be made to happen again in the same way
a small update that fixes a specific problem
a period when the system is not available to users

paras: On Tuesday morning, a serious bug was found in production. The checkout page crashed for some users, and Ana was asked to write the incident report for the stakeholders. She knew that a good report is not a long story; it is short, clear and precise. So she opened with the most important thing: the impact. About fifteen percent of users could not pay, and the problem happened with high frequency during peak hours.

Then she described the symptom in plain words: after the user clicked 'Pay', a blank screen was shown instead of the receipt. The bug was reproducible, so she added the exact steps and a screenshot as evidence. Next, she reported what was done, using the passive voice, because the actions mattered more than the names. The issue was escalated to the backend team, the broken release was rolled back, and a patch was deployed within the hour. Downtime was kept under twenty minutes.

Finally, Ana explained the root cause and the fix. The bug was a regression: a change from the last sprint had broken the payment call. It has now been resolved, and a new test was added so the same regression cannot return. She sent the report to the stakeholders in five short lines. Nobody had to ask her a single follow-up question, and that, for Ana, was the sign of a report done right.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B2 reading, incident reporting for stakeholders

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Ana spends a whole day writing a very long and detailed story about a bug.
Ana writes a short, clear incident report: the impact first, then the symptom and what was done, using the passive voice.
The text explains how to design a checkout page from zero.

items: Ana opened her report with the impact on users, not with a long story.
The text says she opened with 'the most important thing: the impact', and that a good report is 'short, clear and precise'.
The bug could not be reproduced, so Ana had no steps to share.
The text says the opposite: 'The bug was reproducible, so she added the exact steps and a screenshot as evidence.'
Ana used the passive voice to report what was done because the actions mattered more than the names.
The text says: 'using the passive voice, because the actions mattered more than the names.'
The bug was a brand-new problem, not connected to any earlier change.
The text says it was a regression: 'a change from the last sprint had broken the payment call.'

parts: in plain words and noted the high
during peak hours. The issue was escalated, the release was rolled back, and a
was deployed within the hour, so

gapfill.text: Notice the passive voice for the report: the bug WAS FOUND, the issue WAS ESCALATED, a patch WAS DEPLOYED, it HAS BEEN RESOLVED. Form: be + past participle. Use it when the action or the result matters more than who did it.

bank.label: Useful sentences for an incident report
items: A bug was found in production this morning.
About fifteen percent of users are affected.
The issue was escalated to the backend team.
The broken release was rolled back and a patch was deployed.
The bug is reproducible; the steps are attached.
The problem has been resolved and a new test was added.
Downtime was kept under twenty minutes.
The root cause was a regression from the last sprint.

lf.title: Report it in the passive
items: to the backend team. (action, not names)
and a new test was added. (finished, still relevant)

lf.text: Passive = be + past participle. In a report we often drop the 'by...' part, because the action or the result matters more than who did it: 'A patch was deployed' is cleaner than 'Someone deployed a patch.'

items.situation: Report the event to stakeholders: a bug appeared on the checkout page this morning. Use the passive voice.
tips: A bug was found on the checkout page this morning.
A problem was reported on the checkout page during peak hours.

items.situation: State the impact in one clear line. How many users, and how serious?
tips: About fifteen percent of users could not pay.
The impact is high: the checkout was down for some users during peak hours.

items.situation: Report what was done, without naming people. Use the passive voice.
tips: The issue was escalated and the release was rolled back.
A patch was deployed within the hour, so downtime was kept short.

items.situation: Close the report: the bug is fixed. Say it is resolved and cannot come back.
tips: The problem has been resolved and a new test was added.
It has been resolved, so the same regression cannot return.

items: Report a bug in three lines: the impact, the symptom, and one action that was taken. Use the passive voice for the action. Speak from your notes.
Now add the frequency and the root cause. Say the issue was a regression and that a patch was deployed. Keep it concise.
Give the whole incident report out loud, in five short lines, with no notes. Impact first.
A stakeholder asks, 'Is it fixed for good?' Answer clearly: say it has been resolved and how the same regression is prevented.

answerkey.title: Reveal the incident-report toolkit
list: Vocabulary: impact, symptom, frequency, to resolve, regression, reproducible, patch, downtime, to roll back, evidence, concise, vague.
Passive for the event: 'A bug was found in production.'
Passive for the actions: 'The issue was escalated; a patch was deployed.'
Present perfect passive for the result: 'It has been resolved.'
Structure: impact first, then symptom and frequency, then what was done, then root cause and fix. Short and precise, never vague.
answerkey.note: A good report answers the questions before they are asked. Keep it concise.

listenings.text: Hi Ana, this is David from the product side. I just read your incident report about the checkout bug, and I want to say thank you. It was clear and short, exactly what we need for the stakeholders. Two quick things before I forward it up. First, please make the impact even more concrete at the top: how many users were affected, and during which hours. Second, I liked that you used the passive voice for the actions; keep it that way, because the leadership does not need the names, they need to know what was done. One more thing: add a single line at the end that says whether it has been fully resolved or whether we are still watching it. Great work, and let us keep reports this precise from now on.

Hi Ana, it is Priya, your team lead. I want to give you a quick tip on writing incident reports, because you are getting really good at them. The golden rule is simple: lead with the impact, not with the story. A stakeholder reads the first line and must already know how bad it is and who is affected. After that, be precise: give the symptom, the frequency, and whether the bug is reproducible. Then report the actions in the passive voice, so the report stays neutral and clean: the issue was escalated, the release was rolled back, a patch was deployed. And never be vague. Do not write 'some problems were seen'; write 'the payment call failed for fifteen percent of users during peak hours'. Concise and precise, always. That is how you build trust with the people who read your reports.

extra_audio.text: Here is how Ana wrote her incident report. First, she opened with the impact: about fifteen percent of users could not pay. Then she described the symptom, a blank screen after the user clicked Pay. Next, she reported what was done in the passive voice: the issue was escalated and a patch was deployed. After that, she explained the root cause, a regression from the last sprint. Finally, she confirmed that the problem had been resolved and a new test was added.
